main_summary.summary: Virginia Simms George, interviewed by Emilye Crosby on August 24, 2013 at the Northborough, Massachusetts Free Library, recounts her path from Newport News, Virginia to participation in the 1960 Richmond sit-in movement. Raised by her father, a postman, in a segregated but self-contained Black community in Newport News, she attended Virginia Union University beginning in fall 1959. There she befriended Marie Sallasin and Patricia Washington, and in February 1960, under the leadership of ministers Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston, she joined a sit-in targeting the segregated Richmond Room of the Thalhimers department store. Arrested with the group later credited as the Richmond 34, she was spit on by a white man as she left jail with her attorney. Her father forbade her travel to Mississippi, redirecting her toward education. She completed a master's at the University of Maryland, worked for over seventeen years counseling formerly incarcerated and unemployed clients under federal MA-4 contracts in Washington, D.C., Minnesota, and California, and later settled in Northborough as an HR manager. She mentors students at Alfred University's Judson Leadership Center and serves with the Springfield chapter of LINKS Incorporated.
key_themes: Newport News segregation and Black self-sufficiency
main_summary.historical_significance:
George provides first-person testimony from a participant in the February 1960 Richmond sit-in, a campaign that desegregated the Thalhimers department store's Richmond Room and is credited with desegregating downtown Richmond, Virginia. Her account corroborates the leadership role of Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston in organizing Virginia Union students, names attorney Marion L. Poe as an early Black female legal role model in Newport News, and documents the nonviolence training students received in anticipation of arrest. Her recollection of being spit on by a white man outside the jail, and of seeing herself led from the store on the evening news, captures the personal stakes of student-led direct action.

George's testimony also documents the long arc from 1960 sit-in participant to lifelong workplace and civic advocate. Her later HR work on federal MA-4 contracts, her mentoring through LINKS Incorporated and Alfred University, and her workplace advocacy for gender-neutral facilities and transgender employees show how Movement-era commitments shaped a sustained career of advocacy through institutional channels.

chapters.title: Interview Opening
chapters.summary: Emilye Crosby of the Southern Oral History Program at UNC Chapel Hill identifies the interview as part of the Civil Rights History Project, jointly sponsored by the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress's American Folklife Center. The session is recorded at the Northborough, Massachusetts Free Library on August 24, 2013, with George's husband Frederick George and videographer John Bishop present.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording session

chapters.title: Family and Newport News Childhood
chapters.summary: George was born in Newport News, Virginia, named after her grandmother. After her parents separated, she and her brother were raised primarily by their father, a postman who later remarried. When the family moved to a suburban neighborhood, white residents were already leaving; a departing neighbor told her father she would not have sold had she known the family. George recalls a bus incident at age twelve when a white passenger moved away from her, prompting her father's discussion of race and his insistence on education.

chapters.title: Black Community Life in Newport News
chapters.summary: George describes the segregated but self-contained Black community of Newport News: all-Black schools, Dr. Jones and Dr. Tucker's Black-owned drugstore where she worked as a soda-jerk clerk, churches, funeral homes, and the shipyard-driven first-of-the-month influx of sailors that kept her chaperoned by her brother. She references Virginia's massive resistance to Brown but notes Newport News did not close its schools, and recalls her debutante presentation at sixteen and the mentorship of Mary Ann Benz, wife of the first Black urologist in Virginia.

chapters.title: Virginia Union University
chapters.summary: George entered Virginia Union University in fall 1959 to study history with the goal of law school, inspired by attorney Marion L. Poe of Newport News. She describes the Baptist school's strict sign-out rules, her friendships with roommates Marie Sallasin and Patricia Washington (who could pass as white), and an incident in which Washington had her hair cut at a white Richmond barbershop. She traveled to the Penn Relays in Philadelphia and stayed with Washington's family near Philadelphia.

chapters.title: Sit-In Organizing at Virginia Union
chapters.summary: George describes how news of the Greensboro Woolworth sit-ins reached Virginia Union and how ministers Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston led student organizing meetings. Martin Luther King had come to campus. Sherrod and Pinkston prepared the students for arrest, emphasizing nonviolence, and made clear that if either were taken, the other would remain to lead. George recalls Shirley Miller Sherrod as Charles Sherrod's wife.

chapters.title: Arrest at Thalhimers
chapters.summary: George recounts the February 1960 march downtown to Thalhimers, a department store comparable to Macy's where Black customers could shop but were excluded from the Richmond Room tearoom. After picketing and sitting in, she was arrested, taken to jail, and fingerprinted with difficulty because of her long fingernails. Her attorney bailed her out, and as she left the jail a white man stood in front of her and spit on her. That night she saw herself led from the store on the news and called her father.

chapters.title: Aftermath and the Mississippi Decision
chapters.summary: George describes the institutional support she received: no sanctions from Virginia Union, financial and legal support from volunteer lawyers, and welcome at Black churches throughout Richmond. When students planned to travel to Mississippi by bus, her father forbade her to go, telling her he did not want her dead and that she needed to focus on her education. She returned to her studies, taught at Maggie L. Walker High School in Richmond for three years after graduation, then pursued a master's at the University of Maryland.
related_events: Mississippi Freedom Movement bus travel planning

chapters.title: Post-Movement Career
chapters.summary: After her master's at the University of Maryland, George worked for over seventeen years for a private company in Washington, D.C. on inner-city federal MA-4 contracts, counseling Black clients to move off welfare, off drugs, and out of jail and into employment. She relocated through Minnesota, back to Washington, to California, and back to Minnesota before settling in Massachusetts. She frames this work as continuous with the Civil Rights Movement's goal of helping people become viable citizens.

chapters.title: Reunion and Recognition
chapters.summary: George describes never having spoken about the sit-in for decades, even to her husband. Approximately fifty years later, organizers of an anniversary commemoration contacted her. At Virginia Union she saw a plaque with her name and learned that the group of thirty-four students was credited with desegregating the city of Richmond. She reconnected with Marie Sallasin and Patricia Washington at the reunion, having lost contact through Sallasin's military-family travels.

chapters.title: LINKS and Community Work
chapters.summary: George describes her work with the Springfield, Massachusetts chapter of LINKS Incorporated, a national organization of predominantly African American women founded in Philadelphia. The chapter mentors young women, supports a transitional house for formerly incarcerated women recovering from addiction, and tracks members through high school graduation and college, including UMass Amherst. She also chairs the Northborough personnel board, serves on the Assabet Valley Regional School District committee, and works with Alfred University's Judson Leadership Center, named for a deceased sponsor.

chapters.title: Nonviolence and Racism Today
chapters.summary: George reflects on the discipline of nonviolence during the sit-ins, recalling that the students were jeered and called names as they marched and were instructed not to make eye contact. She states that racism remains rampant in the United States and expresses her shock at the election of Barack Obama, attributing congressional obstruction of his presidency to racism. She contrasts her own awareness of race with her son's generation, whom she observes do not see color.

chapters.title: Workplace Advocacy and Closing
chapters.summary: George describes current workplace advocacy at her manufacturing employer, including a campaign for gender-neutral bathrooms near the manufacturing floor and the development of training for managers on transgender workplace issues and inappropriate questions. She frames this as continuous with her Movement-era commitments: understanding differences, valuing differences, and protecting people's ability to make their own choices. The interview closes with thanks from Crosby on behalf of the Civil Rights History Project.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording closing

loc_video.caption: Virginia Simms George oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Northborough, Massachusetts, 2013 August 24
